coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence turn-on detection of fenitrothion using gold nanoparticle quenched fluorescein and its separation using superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticle
coredata.dc:creator: Nebu, John
Anjali Devi, J.S.
Aparna, R.S.
Aswathy, B.
Lekha, G.M.
Sony, George
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a simple and sensitive determination of fenitrothion is developed on the basis of Nanometal Surface Energy Transfer (NSET) of gold nanoparticle on the fluorescence of fluorescein. The developed gold nanoparticle quenched fluorescein system exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity towards fenitrothion in the presence of different pesticides, anions and cations. The fluorescence turn on response of the gold nanoparticle quenched fluorescein probe towards fenitrothion is successfully demonstrated on a paper strip. The real sample analysis was carried out using water samples from different sources and has limit of detection of 6.05 nM, 9.41 nM and 7.84 nM for well water, tap water and river water respectively. However, attempt to simultaneously separate and sense fenitrothion using gold nanoparticle enveloped over superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticle was not successful due to the irreversible quenching of fluorescence of fluorescein by Fe3+ ion present in iron oxide nanoparticle, which inhibits the requisite turn-on fluorescence of the probe. Furthermore, we successfully employed superparamagnetic iron oxide to magnetically separate fenitrothion attached to gold nanoparticle from the contaminated water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoparticle
Superparamagnetism
Pesticide
Fluorescence
Surface Plasmon Resonance
Paper strip assay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of FTN in real sample
Fluorescence turn on response of Au NPs enveloped over Fe3O4 quenched fluorescein with the addition of FTN
Removal of fluorescein@Au NP@FTN from the solution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic representation of the mechanism of quenching of fluorescein by Au NPs and turn-on response of Au NPs quenched fluorescein in the presence of fenitrothion.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectra of (a) Au NPs, (b) fluorescein, (c) Au NPs quenched fluorescein and (d) fenitrothion added Au NPs quenched fluorescein. (Inset: The absorbance spectrum of fenitrothion). (B) Photograph showing the (a) fluorescence of fluorescein & (b) fluorescence turn off response of Au NPs quenched fluorescein. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of fluorescein with increase in concentration of Au NPs from 0 to 2.43 nM. (D) Relative fluorescence intensities (I0/I at 518 nm) of fluorescein with different concentration of Au NPs (concentration of Au NPs from 0 to 2.43 nM). Inset: The Stern-Volmer plot of the fluorescence quenching effect of Au NPs (concentration of Au NPs from 0 to 1.39 nM). (E) TEM image and (F) HRTEM image of fluorescein@Au NPs. Inset (B): SAED pattern of the selected single fluorescein@Au NPs.

(A) The fluorescence turn on response of Au NPs quenched fluorescein probe with increasing concentration of fenitrothion (B) The relative fluorescence intensities (I/I0 at 518 nm) of Au NPs quenched fluorescein system against concentration of fenitrothion (0–14 μM). Inset: The I/I0 linear plot of Au NPs quenched fluorescein system against the concentration of FTN (0.01 μM–4.5 μM). (C) TEM image and (D) HRTEM image of fluorescein@Au NP@FTN. Inset (D): SAED pattern of the selected single fluorescein@Au NP@FTN.

Fluorescence intensity ratios (I/I0 at 518 nm) of Au NPs quenched fluorescein system upon addition of different possible co-existing (A) cations, (B) anions and (C) other pesticides (CA – cumulative addition). (D) Photograph showing fluorescein turn on response of Au NPs quenched fluorescein system upon addition of fenitrothion. Photograph depicting the fluorescence response of different possible co-existing (E) cations, (F) anions and (G) other pesticides on Au NPs quenched fluorescein upon irradiation with UV radiation having λmax = 365 nm. (Interferents order is maintained as same in the selectivity studies).

Fluorescence intensity ratios (I/I0 at 518 nm) of Au NPs quenched fluorescein in the presence of different co-existing species ((A) cations, (B) anions & (C) other pesticide) at concentration of 4.5 μM and subsequent addition of fenitrothion (4.5 μM). (CA- Cumulative addition). (D) Paper strip showing (a) fluorescence of fluorescein, (b) fluorescence turn off by Au NPs quenched fluorescein and (c) fluorescence turn on by fenitrothion added Au NPs quenched fluorescein upon irradiation with UV radiation (λmax = 365 nm). (E) Paper strip showing the fluorescence response of (a) FTN, (b) 2,4-dichlorophenoxy acetic acid, (c) diuron, (d) malathion, (e) chloropyrifos and (f) dichlorvos on Au NPs quenched fluorescein system upon irradiation with UV radiation at 365 nm.

(A) Chemical structure of fenitrothion. (B) The time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) life time analysis of fluorescein, Au Nps quenched fluorescein and fenitrothion added Au Nps quenched fluorescein. (B) The UV–vis absorbance spectrum of Au NPs and emission spectrum of fluorescein indicating the spectral overlap between the two favoring NSET. (C) The UV–vis absorbance spectrum of Au NPs and absorbance spectrum of fluorescein showing some overlapped region favoring secondary inner filter effect.

(A) Fluorescence response of Fe3O4 nanoparticle enveloped over Au NPs quenched fluorescein by the addition of increasing concentration of fenitrothion. (B) Fluorescence spectrum of fluorescein in the solution (fluorescein@Au NP@FTN) (a) before and (b) after magnetic separation using Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Inset: Absorption spectrum of fenitrothion in the solution (fluorescein@Au NP@FTN-Fe3O4) (c) before and (d) after magnetic separation. (C) Photograph representing the magnetic separation of the residue (fluorescein@Au NP@FTN-Fe3O4). (D) TEM image containing aggregated particles of Au NPs and Fe3O4 in fluorescein@Au NP@FTN-Fe3O4 and (E) HRTEM image showing the lattice fringes of Au NPs and Fe3O4 in fluorescein@Au NP@FTN-Fe3O4. Inset: SAED pattern of Au NPs and Fe3O4 in fluorescein@Au NP@FTN-Fe3O4.
The fluorescence turn on studies of FTN in real samples from different water sources.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence turn-on detection of fenitrothion using gold nanoparticle quenched fluorescein and its separation using superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticle

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences, University of Kerala, Kariavattom, Trivandrum 695581, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences, University of Kerala, Kariavattom, Trivandrum 695581, Kerala, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The graphical abstract depicting the mechanism of Nanometal Surface Energy Transfer (NSET) of the quenching of fluorescence of fluorescein by gold nanoparticle and its turn on by fenitrothion.

In this work, a simple and sensitive determination of fenitrothion is developed on the basis of Nanometal Surface Energy Transfer (NSET) of gold nanoparticle on the fluorescence of fluorescein. The developed gold nanoparticle quenched fluorescein system exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity towards fenitrothion in the presence of different pesticides, anions and cations. The fluorescence turn on response of the gold nanoparticle quenched fluorescein probe towards fenitrothion is successfully demonstrated on a paper strip. The real sample analysis was carried out using water samples from different sources and has limit of detection of 6.05 nM, 9.41 nM and 7.84 nM for well water, tap water and river water respectively. However, attempt to simultaneously separate and sense fenitrothion using gold nanoparticle enveloped over superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticle was not successful due to the irreversible quenching of fluorescence of fluorescein by Fe3+ ion present in iron oxide nanoparticle, which inhibits the requisite turn-on fluorescence of the probe. Furthermore, we successfully employed superparamagnetic iron oxide to magnetically separate fenitrothion attached to gold nanoparticle from the contaminated water.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticide is a global inevitable evil. Fenitrothion, O,O-dimethyl O-(3-methyl-4-nitrophenyl) phosphorothioate [1] (FTN), an organophosphorus insecticide, acaricide and acetylcholine esterase inhibitor, which is extensively used for pest control in wheat, rice, cereals, fruits, stored grains and vegetables. FTN potentially harms humans by inhibiting the activity of acetylcholinesterases (AChE) at critical neuronal sites [2–4]. It is an endocrine disrupting substance causing delayed neuropathy [3,5]. Human survivors of occupational or environmental exposure to FTN suffer with an ‘intermediate syndrome’ having delayed muscle weakness [6]. Epidemiological studies reminds that exposure to organophosphorus pesticide like FTN may cause increased DNA damage and decreased concentration in human sperm transcending the lethal effect to next generation [7]. Studies on rats reveals that FTN causes deleterious effects in its sperm and testes [8]. The World Health Organization (WHO) specification for public health pesticides warns about the toxicity of FTN to honey bees, non-target arthropods and aquatic organisms (evaluation report 35/2009). Previous investigation on FTN by Sancho et al. found that it can bioaccumulates in the liver, muscle, blood and brain of the exposed fish at static water bodies [9].

Southam et al. studied the target and off-target toxicities of FTN to Rutilus rutilus [2]. Suzuki et al. detected fatty acid amide hydrolase as a potential target for organophosphate pesticide-elicited spermototoxicity [7]. Otale et al. developed green onion and cabbage powder certified reference materials for the determination of organophosphorus and pyrethroid pesticides [10]. Roepcke et al. analyzed phosphorothionate pesticides with acetylcholinesterase biosensor detection [11]. Yan et al. developed a silica mesochannels and surfactant micelles based material for the extraction and electrochemical analysis of pesticides and nitroaromatic explosives [12]. Kumar et al. developed a luminescent nanocrystal metal organic framework for sensing of organophosphate pesticides containing nitro group [13]. Govindaswamy et al. developed silver@graphene nanoribbons based screen printed electrode for the detection of methyl parathion [14].

The potential environmental threat due to the extensive use of FTN in crops demands an urgent need to develop simple and rapid sensing methods for its detection in real samples. Although, currently employed methods like gas chromatography, electrochemical sensing methods, high performance liquid chromatography and mass selective detectors have many advantages but each one have its own limitations [15]. In addition to this, the need for specificity of FTN over other similar compounds enhances the demand for a robust sensing probe. On the other hand, fluorescence sensing based on fluorophores is simple, less expensive and easy-to-handle.

Shams et al. studied the electrochemical sensing of fenitrothion in water by using gold nanoparticle/ethylene diamine-reduced graphene oxide. Here ethylenediamine is used to reduce graphene oxide to reduced graphene oxide and also act as growth points for gold nanoparticles [16]. Dasary et al. studied gold nanoparticle based SERS probe for the detection of trinitrotoluene (TNT). Here TNT and cysteine forms Meisenheimer complex. The gold nanoparticle undergoes aggregation via electrostatic interaction with the Meisenheimer complex of TNT. The hot spot formed results in the enhancement of the intensity of Raman signal [17]. Yan et al. studied the glutathione reductase activity with gold nanoparticle and graphene quantum dots. Here glutathione reductase catalysis activity was evaluated based on Forster resonance energy resonance between graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles [18]. Kang et al. investigated the quenching effect of gold nanoparticle on the fluorescence of a near infrared fluorophore, cypate. Here cypate is placed on the outermost layer of the polymer coated gold nanoparticle [19].

In the present work, we report the selective detection of FTN based on the gold nanoparticle quenched fluorescein probe (fluorescein@Au NPs) (Scheme 1 ). Here, the fluorescence of fluorescein having emission wavelength at 518 nm was quenched by using gold nanoparticle (Au NPs) having absorbance maximum at 520 nm. The results showed that fluorescein@Au NPs probe get selectively switched on with FTN containing sulphur atom on one end and nitro group on the other end. Here, it is demonstrated that fluorescein@Au NPs probe is a suitable probe for FTN detection in different water samples from various sources. With an intention to simultaneously sense and separate FTN from contaminated water, magnetic separation via gold nanoparticle coated over superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticle (Fe3O4) was attempted. This study also investigated the magnetic separation ability of the superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticle for separating FTN attached to Au NPs from the contaminated water.

All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade or better. Hydrogentetrachloroaurate trihydrate (HAuCl4.3H2O), fluorescein, fenitrothion, diuron, malathion, chlorpyrifos, dichlorvos and 2,4-dichlorophenoxy acetic acid were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Mercury(II) chloride, sodium chloride, cobalt(II) chloride, copper(II) chloride, potassium chloride, zinc chloride, ammonium chloride, calcium chloride, magnesium chloride, nickel(II) chloride, lead(II) chloride, trisodium phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium chloride, sodium carbonate, sodium bicarbonate, sodium acetate, sodium sulphate, sodium fluoride, sodium thiosulphate and sodium iodide were obtained from Merck. Absolute ethyl alcohol was purchased from Changshu Yangyuan Chemical, China. Millipore water was used in all experiments.

Fluorescence spectra were obtained from a FP-750 fluorescence spectrofluorometer (Jasco, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained from a Lambda 365 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, America). Transmission electron microscope images were obtained from HRTEM apparatus of JEOL JEM 2100 operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The x-ray photoelectron spectra were collected from Axis ULTRA XPS instrument.

The Au NPs synthesized by the reduction of HAuCl4.3H2O with sodium citrate was based on a previously reported method. Briefly, about 25 mL HAuCl4.3H2O (1 mM) was mixed with 2.5 mL sodium citrate (38.8 mM) with vigorous stirring (600 rpm) for 10 min. The mixture was boiled (100 °C) under stirring and then the hot plate was turned off. A color change was observed within 10 min and the stirring was continued for 20 min. The concentration of the synthesized Au NPs solution was estimated to be ⁓8.0 nM according to Beers law and this solution was stored at 4 °C for further use [20].

To detect the quenching effect of Au NPs on fluorescein, 3 mL of 0.1 mM fluorescein was mixed with different amount of the synthesized Au NPs (concentration from 0.053 nM to 2.43 nM). The mixture was shaken well and equilibrated for 3 min to measure the fluorescence emission spectra with an excitation wavelength of 485 nm. To get Stern-Volmer plot, 3 mL of 0.1 mM fluorescein was mixed with different amount of Au NPs to get an effective concentration of Au NPs from 0.053 nM to 1.39 nM.

To quantitatively detect FTN, 3 mL of fluorescein (0.1 mM) was mixed with 1.39 nM Au NPs. The mixture was shaken thoroughly and equilibrated for 3 min. To this, different concentration of FTN [dissolved in minimum amount (5 mL) of ethyl alcohol and 20 mL water] was added to get an effective concentration from 0.01 μM to 14 μM, and the mixture was shaken well and equilibrated for 3 min. The linear plot is obtained at concentration from 0.01 μM to 4.5 μM and hence 4.5 μM concentration of FTN is used for further studies.

To investigate the selectivity, different cations of their corresponding chlorides such as Hg2+, Na+, Co2+, K+, Zn2+, NH4 +, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, different anions of their sodium salts such as PO4 3−, HPO4 2−, Cl−, CO3 2−, CH3COO−, SO4 2−, F−, S2O3 2−, I− and ethanolic solution of pesticides such as 2,4-dichlorophenoxy acetic acid, diuron, malathion, chlorpyrifos, fenitrothion and dichlorvos at the same concentration of 4.5 μM were individually investigated and recorded.

The interference of FTN was studied with different pesticides, cations, anions and cumulative addition of all pesticides. In brief, 3 mL of 0.1 mM fluorescein was mixed with 1.39 nM Au NPs. It is then shaken thoroughly and treated with 4.5 μM anions, cations and pesticides separately. Here, cumulative addition of anions, cations as well as pesticides were also performed (effective concentration of each species = 4.5 μM). It is then treated with FTN separately (effective concentration = 4.5 μM).

The paper strip assay was carried out with fluorescein@Au NPs probe. The Whatman No. 40 filter paper was cut into pieces (5 cm × 1.5 cm) and dipped in fluorescein (0.1 mM) and dried for 30 min. It is then soaked in Au NPs (4.5 μM) and dried again for 1 h. This dried filter paper is used as the probe for paper strip assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of FTN in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: Water from different sources such as river, tap water and well water were collected, spiked with FTN (effective concentration of 0.50, 0.67, 0.83 μM) and the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

The superparamagnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized using previously reported method with slight modification [21]. To 25 mL of 1 mM aqueous solution of arginine, about 10 mL of 80 mM FeSO4 (NH4)2SO4.6H2O was added under magnetic stirring (600 rpm). After 1 min, 5 mL of 25% ammonia was added with stirring for about 1 h at 25 °C. The Fe3O4 nanoparticle formed were magnetically separated, and dried for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence turn on response of Au NPs enveloped over Fe3O4 quenched fluorescein with the addition of FTN
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the quenching effect of Au NPs enveloped over Fe3O4 on fluorescein, 3 mL of 0.1 mM fluorescein was mixed with Au NPs-Fe3O4, in which the mixture contains Au NPs of concentration 1.39 nM and 50 μL synthesized solution of Fe3O4. This probe solution was subjected to turn on studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal of fluorescein@Au NP@FTN from the solution
ce:section.ce:para: The removal of fluorescein@Au NP@FTN from the solution using arginine reduced Fe3O4 was investigated as follows: to the tested solution of fluorescein@Au NP@FTN containing 3 mL of 0.1 mM fluorescein, 1.39 nM Au NPs and 4.5 μM FTN, add 200 mg dried Fe3O4 and equilibrated for 1 h after sonicating for about 10 min. Then a magnet was employed to separate the electrostatically adsorbed pristine fluorescein and fenitrothion attached to (i) gold nanoparticle (ii) fluorescein bonded gold nanoparticle, on magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticle.

The absorption spectra of Au NPs shows a strong Surface Plasmon Resonance absorption at 520 nm, which indicates the formation of nanoparticles having diameter of ⁓20 nm (Fig. 1 (A)) [22]. As shown in Figure S2(A), the TEM image of Au NPs reveals well defined spherical shape with a diameter of 20.0 ± 2.0 nm, which are distributed over a large domain without any aggregation. HRTEM image of Au NPs (Figure S2(B)) having spacing between lattice fringes measures at 0.20 nm corresponding to the (220) plane of the nanoparticle [23]. Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the sample indicates the polycrystalline nature of Au NPs. The size distribution and hydrodynamic diameter of the citrate capped Au NPs as shown in Figure S1(A) indicates a mean diameter of 37.38 ± 3.10 nm. The absorbance maximum of fluorescein was observed at wavelength of 485 nm, which is employed as the excitation wavelength for further studies (Fig. 1(A)).

To construct the sensing probe at nonfluorescence off state, the fluorescein was treated with Au NPs (Fig. 1(B)). As indicated in Fig. 1(C), the fluorescence intensity of fluorescein was quenched gradually with the increasing concentration of Au NPs (0.053 nM to 2.43 nM). It is noted that 1.39 nM of Au NPs would induce ⁓75% quenching of the initial fluorescence intensity of fluorescence and this concentration of Au NPs is used to explore further turn-on behavior of the quenched probe. As shown in Fig. 1(A), the UV–vis absorption spectrum of fluorescein@Au NPs show peaks at 491 nm corresponds to the absorbance wavelength of fluorescein and 524 nm corresponds to the absorbance wavelength of Au NPs. The red shift observed for both peaks attributed to the interaction of fluorescein molecules with Au NPs. The TEM image of fluorescein@Au NPs reveals the diameter of the nanoparticle is about 20 ± 4 nm (Fig. 1(E)). HRTEM image and SAED pattern indicates the stability of the Au NPs as well as its polycrystalline nature in the presence of fluorescein having polyphenolic groups, which may act as capping agent (Fig. 1(F)). The DLS measurement reveals the hydrodynamic diameter of the fluorescein@Au NPs is about 39.09 ± 8.30 nm (Figure S1 (B)). The plot of relative fluorescence intensity of I0/I at 518 nm as a function of Au NPs concentration is shown in Fig. 1(D). The calibration curve possesses a linear range from 0.053 nM to 1.39 nM (I0/I = 4.29[Au NPs] + 0.987, R2 = 0.992) (Inset of Fig. 1(D)). The Stern-Volmer studies on the quenching phenomena reveals that the plot has a Stern-Volmer constant, Ksv = 4.29.

The fluorescence turn on detection of FTN was carried out with fluorescein@Au NPs. As shown in Fig. 2 (A), with increasing concentration of FTN from 0 μM to 14 μM, the fluorescence intensity of fluorescein@Au NPs gradually increases. The relative fluorescence intensity (I/I0) of fluorescein@Au NPs and fluorescein@Au NP@FTN shows sigmoid curve (Fig. 2(B)) having a linear range from 0.01 μM to 4.5 μM (I/I0 = 0.259 [FTN] + 1.09, R2 = 0.997) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 31.2 nM (Inset Figure). The LOD (n = 3, number of experiments) is calculated using the equation 3σ/k (σ = 0.0027 and k = 0.259).

The TEM image of fluorescein@Au NPs @FTN (Fig. 2(C)) shows that the turn–on ensemble consists of Au NPs linked to each other with FTN as a linker molecule between the adjacent nanoparticle. Here, it is possible because of the presence of sulphur atom on one end and NO2 group on the other end of FTN, which form head-to-tail interaction of FTN with Au NPs. The HRTEM image of fluorescein@Au NPs @FTN reveals the lattice fringes of Au NPs and the interaction between adjacent Au NPs through FTN (Fig. 2(D)). Inset of Fig. 2(B) shows the SAED pattern of Au NPs present in fluorescein@Au NPs. The DLS measurement of fluorescein@Au NPs @FTN as shown in Figure S1(C) indicates the hydrodynamic diameter of 64.5 ± 41.2, which further confirms the interaction between Au NPs in the presence of FTN.

The as-prepared fluorescein@Au NPs was treated with different cations, anions and pesticides and the fluorescein intensity ratios of the probe at a concentration of 4.5 μM were recorded. As shown in Fig. 3 (A)–(C), only FTN recovered the fluorescence of fluorescein present in Au NPs-quenched fluorescein. All possible co-existing cations, anions and other pesticides did not induce significant fluorescence turn on response. Here the cumulative addition (CA) of possible co-existing cations, anions and other pesticides devoid of FTN does not induce a significant turn-on effect. The photograph depicting the fluorescence response of different cations, anions and pesticides clearly indicates the selectivity of the FTN to turn on the fluorescence of Au NPs quenched fluorescein (Fig. 3(E–G).

To investigate the interference of the probe, fluorescence response was analysed in the presence of different cations, anions and pesticides (Fig. 4 (A)–(C)). The co-existence of each ions/pesticides with FTN proves that the turn on response of it remains unperturbed. In order to compare the activity of different pesticides, we studied the possible interaction of FTN with Au NPs. In contrast to other pesticides, the positive response of FTN is due to the presence of –NO2 group and sulphur atom present in it [24–26]. In malathion and chlorpyrifos, sulphur atom is present but lacks NO2 group limited its possibility for head-to-tail interaction with Au NPs. The possible interaction of Au NPs with NO2 group is chemical adsorption at room temperature and that with sulphur atom arises due to the chemical affinity of it with Au NPs (Fig. 5 (A)) [27,28]. The electron density of sulphur atom is bonded to the oxidized Au species present in Au NPs [29–31]. The turn on response of FTN was not affected for cumulative presence of different cations, anions or pesticides. The paper strip analysis of fluorescein, fluorescein@Au NPs and fluorescein@Au NP@FTN using Whatman No. 40 filter paper proves a distinguishable quenching and turn-on response, which ensures the onsite detection of FTN (Fig. 4(D)). The paper strip depicting the fluorescence response of (a) FTN, (b) 2,4-dichlorophenoxy acetic acid, (c) diuron, (d) malathion, (e) chloropyrifos and (f) dichlorvos on Au NPs quenched fluorescein system upon irradiation with UV radiation at 365 nm is shown in Fig. 4E. It indicates the practical feasibility of the method for detecting FTN.

The absorption spectrum of Au NPs (λmax = 520 nm) overlaps with the emission spectrum of fluorescein (λmax = 518 nm) (Fig. 5(C)). Furthermore, the absorption spectrum of fluorescein (λmax = 485 nm) also overlaps with the absorption spectrum of Au NPs (λmax = 520 nm) (Fig. 5(D)). The various possible mechanism of fluorescence attenuation of fluorescein in the presence of Au NPs can be attributed to the non-radiative energy transfer from the dye to the Au NPs, scattering of radiative photons by Au NPs and secondary inner filter effect caused by the (i) possible reabsorption of fluorescence & (ii) absorption of photons by Au NPs [32,33]. The size of the Au NPs of ⁓ 20 nm is unfavorable for scattering and absorption of radiation by Au NPs [34]. From the TEM image of fluorescein@Au NPs (Fig. 1(E)), it is clear that there is no complex formation between Au NPs and fluorescein, which rules out the involvement of static quenching [35,36]. The fluorescence decay curve of fluorescein follows a triexponential pattern with an average fluorescence life time (ꚍav) calculated to be 5.03 ns (Fig. 5(B)). The life time of fluorescence in fluorescein decreases from 5.03 ns to 4.09 ns, occurs after the addition of Au NPs, attributed to non-radiative energy transfer from fluorescein to Au NPs favoring dynamic quenching. It occurs due to the diffusive interaction between the dye and the metal nanoparticle [33,37]. In order to explain the dynamic quenching of a fluorophore, the mechanism based on Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is commonly employed [18,35,38–40]. But FRET occurs through electromagnetic coupling of two dipoles and it is limited to a detectable distance of <10 nm [38]. In contrast to FRET mechanism explaining the donor-acceptor interaction in dye-dye mixture, the dye-metal nanoparticle system follows nanometal surface energy transfer (NSET), in which the interaction between the two is through electromagnetic field generated by the fluorophore dipole and the conduction band electrons of Au NPs. In NSET, the Au NPs have large surface and is proportional to d−4, where d is the distance between the dipoles, and thus the possibility of energy transfer is more compared to FRET [34,41–44]. In addition to NSET, another probability for quenching is due to secondary inner filter effect caused by the complimentary absorption of co-existing Au NPs in the vicinity of fluorescein and by reabsorption of fluorescence [45,46].

The TCSPC analysis of fluorescein follows a tri-exponential pathway having one major life time component 4.86 ns having relative amplitude of 98.34% (Table S1). This characteristic life time of pristine fluorescein was diminished substantially to a relative amplitude of 4.52% in Au NPs bounded fluorescein, followed by the appearance of a new decay pathway of 3.98 ns with a relative amplitude of 92.75%. The TCSPC analysis of fluorescein@Au NP@FTN system exhibit two major life time components of 4.46 ns and 99.1 μs with relative amplitude of 42.75% and 55.56% respectively, corresponding to the presence of still Au NPs bonded fluorescein and fluorescein freed by FTN. The average fluorescence life time of fluorescein increases to 99.1 ns in fluorescein@Au NP@FTN system. The increase in life time of fluorescein in fluorescein@Au NP@FTN system is indicative of the enhanced stability and reduced solvent interaction of the bonded fluorescein in the presence of FTN [47].

The zeta potential of citrate capped Au NPs is −4.8 mV and fluorescein is -36.7 mV. Here the zeta potential of fluorescein@Au NPs is −22 mV, which indicates that when Au NPs is added to fluorescein, the dye imparts its negative charge to Au NPs, thereby stabilized the nanoparticle. After adding FTN to fluorescein@Au NPs, the zeta potential of fluorescein@Au NP@FTN becomes −3.7 mV. This is due to the removal of fluorescein from the vicinity of Au NPs by FTN. Here the surface area of Au NPs decreases and the fluorescein dye, which are not in the vicinity of aggregated Au NPs, shows turn on fluorescence.

The real sample analysis was carried out with spiked FTN using fluorescein@Au NPs in water samples collected from different sources (Table 1 ). The limit of detection (LOD) and recovery percentage of FTN in well water are 6.05 nM and 109.25–112.71% respectively. For tap water, the LOD and recovery percentage are 9.41 and 99.9 to 102.46%. For river water, the LOD and recovery percentage are 7.84 and 105.38 to 109.12%.

With an intention to impart magnetic concentration and separation of FTN from affected water, we tried to envelope Au NPs over superparamagnetic Fe3O4 core. Here we used arginine reduced superparamagnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticle having positive zeta potential. It is observed that even though the fluorescence property of fluorescein gets quenched by the addition of Au NP-Fe3O4 mixture, the probe does not elicit turn on response by the addition of FTN (Fig. 6 (A)). It indicates that the presence of Fe3+ in superparamagnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticle, which is renowned as a universal quencher of fluorescence, quenched the fluorescence of fluorescein irreversibly.

After fluorescence turn on sensing of FTN with pristine fluorescein@Au NPs, we try to separate the fluorescein@Au NP@FTN from the water by adding Fe3O4 into it. As shown in Figure S2(C), TEM image demonstrate that Fe3O4 nanopartilce having a diameter of 11.3 ± 2.1 nm and the HRTEM image consists of lattice fringes of Fe3O4 (Figure S2(D)). The hydrodynamic diameter shows an average diameter of 93.86 ± 78.35 nm and it clearly indicates the formation of aggregated particles. The zeta potential measurement of Fe3O4 is +7.6, which is suitable for electrostatic attraction of it with negatively charged fluorescein@Au NP@FTN. The TEM image and HRTEM image of Fe3O4 added fluorescein@Au NP@FTN mixture (fluorescein@Au NP@FTN-Fe3O4) indicates the presence of both Au NPs and Fe3O4 nanoparticles in their aggregated forms (Fig. 6(D)). Inset figure of 6(E) consists of the SAED pattern of both Au NPs and Fe3O4 nanoparticle. The zeta potential measurement of -3.7 for fluorescein@Au NP@FTN results in the adsorption of it on Fe3O4. Fig. 6(C) represents the photograph of magnetic separation of fluorescein@Au NP@FTN after introducing superparamagnetic Fe3O4 into the mixture. It is observed that the solution becomes clear within 20 s, which attests the magnetic separation ability of Fe3O4 nanoparticle to separate FTN bonded over (i) Au NPs and (ii) fluorescein@Au NPs. Moreover, it can separate pristine fluorescein having negative zeta potential of -36.7 and simultaneously quenched the fluorescence of fluorescein by virtue of the Fe3+ ions present over Fe3O4 nanoparticle. The clear solution lacks fluorescence property and absence of fluorescence peak of fluorescein indicates the removal of pristine fluorescein from the solution (Fig. 6(B)). The absence of absorption spectrum of FTN in clear solution (Inset of Fig. 6(B)) attests the complete magnetic separation of FTN from the solution of fluorescein@Au NP@FTN.

To analyze the magnetically separated residue, XPS analysis was done to explore the elemental composition. The two peaks centered at 709.75 and 710.74 eV, attributed to the Fe(II)-O and Fe(III)-O bonds present in magnetite (Figure S3 (A)). The broad peak observed at 723.5 eV indicates the binding energy corresponding to 2p1/2 orbital. The peaks located at 83.41 and 87.08 eV corresponds to Au 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 indicating the existence of Au(0) species in the residue (Figure S3(B)). The C 1 s, O 1 s peaks arises due to the presence of citrate ions and fluorescein present in the residue (Figure S3(C & D)). The S 2p dominant peak at 162.5 eV indicates the sulphur atom bound to gold attributed to the strong interaction between Au NPs and sulphur atom present in FTN (Figure S3(E)). The P 2p peak for phosphorus at 133 eV indicates the metal-phosphate bond (Figure S3(F). The results attests the successful removal of fluorescein, FTN attached to Au NPs and fluorescein@Au NPs using Fe3O4, which throws light for the purification of water contaminated with FTN.
The Au NPs undergo aggregation in the presence of FTN. Hence the method is irreversible in nature, which is the major limitation of the study.

In this work, a highly selective and sensitive FTN sensor is developed for the determination of FTN in well water, tap water and river water using fluorescein@Au NPs. The Au NPs, fluorescein@Au NPs and fluorescein@Au NP@FTN are prepared through wet chemical method and characterized by TEM, UV–vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, TCSPC, DLS and XPS techniques. The presence of Au NPs with fluorescein leads to fluorescence quenching and by the addition of FTN, the fluorescence gets switched on. The fluorescein@Au NPs has sensitivity, selectivity towards FTN over other pesticides and has detection limit in the nanomolar range. The superparamagnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticle can be coated with silica shell before enveloping with Au NPs, which may avoid leaching of Fe3+ ions out of the core resulting only reversible quenching, is under consideration for future studies in the simultaneous sensing and separation of FTN.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Head, Department of Chemistry, University of Kerala, Kariavattom Campus, Thiruvananthapuram for pursuing the platform to conduct the research. The authors also thank the Director, SICC, University of Kerala, Kariavattom campus, Thiruvananthapuram; Director, SAIF-STIC-CUSAT, Kochi; DST-SAIF, M.G. University, Kottayam. The author N.J. acknowledge support for this work by University Grants Commission, Bangalore, India through the teacher fellowship (F.No.FIP/12th plan/KLMG035, TF: 03) under faculty development programme during XIIth plan period.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.08.153.
